---
title: "Who's Actually Building Cycling Infrastructure?"
author: "Sam Lumley"
date: "2026-04-20"
format:
  html:
    theme: flatly
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

import pandas as pd
import geopandas as gpd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches
from matplotlib.colors import LinearSegmentedColormap
import matplotlib.ticker as mticker
import contextily as ctx
import numpy as np
from pathlib import Path

DATA = Path("data/processed")
FIGS = Path("figures")
FIGS.mkdir(exist_ok=True)

# Consistent city colours used throughout
CITY_COLOURS = {
    "Victoria":  "#2196F3",
    "Montreal":  "#4CAF50",
    "Toronto":   "#FF9800",
}

plt.rcParams.update({
    "font.family": "sans-serif",
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "figure.facecolor": "white",
})
```

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
This is preliminary work based on openly available data.
It's not an official release and should not be cited as such. ✌️ 
:::

## 🚲 Intro

Bike infrastructure is one of the cheapest ways a city can
expand who can get around — but only one kind actually works.

Painted lanes on busy roads do almost nothing for ridership —
[the research on this is settled](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tbs.2023.100736){target="_blank"}.
What moves the needle is **high-comfort** infrastructure:
physically separated cycle tracks, multi-use paths, and local street 
bikeways. Places where you don't have to negotiate with a moving car.

Between 2019 and 2024, Canadian cities added hunfreds of kilometres
of cycling infrastructure. Not all of it was equal.

Using the
**[Canadian Cycling Network Database](https://www150.statcan.gc.ca/n1/pub/23-26-0004/232600042024001-eng.htm){target="_blank"}**
and the **[Can-BICS](https://www.canada.ca/en/public-health/services/reports-publications/health-promotion-chronic-disease-prevention-canada-research-policy-practice/vol-40-no-9-2020/canbics-classification-system-naming-convention-cycling-infrastructure.html){target="_blank"}**
classification framework,
we measured how access to high-comfort infrastructure changed
across three cities: Victoria, Montreal, and Toronto.

**TL;DR:** Victoria punches way above its weight.
Montreal has made real gains.
Toronto has been sluggish — despite being 
Canada's largest city.

---

## 📊 Data

The analysis uses two national snapshots of Canadian cycling
infrastructure:

- **[2019](https://health-infobase.canada.ca/datalab/bicycling-infrastructure.html){target="_blank"}**
  — baseline network, published by the Public Health Agency of Canada
- **[2024](https://www150.statcan.gc.ca/n1/pub/23-26-0004/232600042024001-eng.htm){target="_blank"}**
  — From Statistics Canada

Each dataset is a collection of line segments — one per stretch of
infrastructure — with attributes describing the type of facility.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-raw-counts
#| tbl-cap: "Raw segment counts and total kilometres by city and year."

# TODO: replace with real data once processed
# Expected columns: city, year, n_segments, total_km, high_comfort_km

summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "City":            ["Victoria", "Victoria", "Montreal", "Montreal", "Toronto", "Toronto"],
    "Year":            [2019, 2024, 2019, 2024, 2019, 2024],
    "Total km":        [None, None, None, None, None, None],
    "High-comfort km": [None, None, None, None, None, None],
    "% High-comfort":  [None, None, None, None, None, None],
})
# Uncomment when data is ready:
# summary = pd.read_csv(DATA / "network_summary.csv")

summary.style \
    .format({"Total km": "{:.0f}", "High-comfort km": "{:.0f}", "% High-comfort": "{:.1f}%"},
            na_rep="—") \
    .set_properties(**{"text-align": "right"}) \
    .set_table_styles([{"selector": "th", "props": [("text-align", "left")]}]) \
    .hide(axis="index")
```

---

## 🏷️ Classification is hard

Here's a problem that doesn't get enough air time in cycling research:
municipalities have wildly inconsistent names for their infrastructure.

One city's "bike lane" is a painted stripe in the door zone.
Another's is a fully protected cycle track with a physical barrier.
A third calls the same thing a "cycling corridor,"
or something that doesn't map cleanly to any standard category at all.

This matters because the raw data reflects whatever label
each municipality chose to use —
not a consistent, objective description of what was actually built.

### How we resolved it

We used **[Can-BICS](URL_TODO){target="_blank"}**
(Canadian Bikeway Comfort and Safety) as a common framework.
Can-BICS classifies cycling infrastructure into three comfort tiers
based on physical separation from motor traffic:
*high*, *medium*, and *low*.
This analysis focuses on the high-comfort tier —
the facilities with genuine physical separation
that [research shows actually shift ridership](URL_TODO){target="_blank"}.

To apply Can-BICS consistently, we verified classifications
using **Google Street View**, checking ambiguous segments
in each city against what was actually on the ground.

![*Does this count? A Montreal bike lane. The post is load-bearing.*](images/montrealbikelane_2.jpg){width=100%}

---

## 📍 How do we measure spatial access?

We measure access at the **dissemination block (DB)** level —
Statistics Canada's smallest geographic unit with population counts.
There are thousands of them per city.
For each one, we ask: **is there high-comfort infrastructure nearby?**

```{python}
#| label: fig-method-diagram
#| fig-cap: "Each dissemination block centroid is buffered by 300 m (straight-line distance). A block has access if any high-comfort segment falls within that buffer."

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))

# Draw a stylised block grid
for x in range(5):
    for y in range(4):
        rect = plt.Rectangle((x, y), 0.9, 0.9, linewidth=0.5,
                              edgecolor="#bbb", facecolor="#f5f5f5")
        ax.add_patch(rect)

# A high-comfort segment running diagonally
ax.plot([0.5, 4.5], [0.5, 3.5], color="#2196F3", linewidth=3,
        label="High-comfort segment", zorder=3)

# Show two centroids and their buffers
centroids = [(0.45, 2.45), (2.45, 0.45)]
colours   = ["#4CAF50", "#FF9800"]
labels    = ["Within 300 m → access = 1", "Outside 300 m → access = 0"]

for (cx, cy), colour, label in zip(centroids, colours, labels):
    circle = plt.Circle((cx, cy), 0.8, color=colour, alpha=0.2)
    ax.add_patch(circle)
    ax.plot(cx, cy, "o", color=colour, markersize=7, zorder=4)
    ax.annotate(label, (cx, cy), xytext=(cx + 0.15, cy + 0.5),
                fontsize=9, color=colour,
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-", color=colour, lw=0.8))

ax.set_xlim(-0.2, 5.2)
ax.set_ylim(-0.2, 4.5)
ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.axis("off")
ax.set_title("300 m buffer access method", fontsize=12, pad=10)

blue_line = mpatches.Patch(color="#2196F3", label="High-comfort segment")
ax.legend(handles=[blue_line], loc="upper left", frameon=False, fontsize=9)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(FIGS / "method_diagram.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()
```

For each DB centroid $i$, we compute a binary access indicator:

$$
a_i = \begin{cases}
  1 & \text{if } d(i,\ \text{nearest high-comfort segment}) \leq 300\text{ m} \\
  0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Then roll it up to a city-level **population-weighted access share**:

$$
A = \frac{\sum_i p_i \cdot a_i}{\sum_i p_i}
$$

where $p_i$ is the 2021 census population of block $i$.
Dense blocks count more: a gain in a high-rise neighbourhood
outweighs the same gain in a low-density suburb. 

### Why 300 m?

300 m is roughly a 4-minute walk,
and a [well-established threshold](URL_TODO){target="_blank"} in transport geography
for "close enough to use."

We also tested 200 m and 500 m to confirm the story doesn't change
with the threshold. It doesn't —
city rankings are consistent across all three distances.

---

### 🏆 Victoria: the standout

Victoria is the smallest city in this analysis.
It also has the most dramatic trajectory.

<!-- TODO: populate with real numbers once analysis runs -->

Between 2019 and 2024, the share of Victoria residents within 300 m
of high-comfort infrastructure rose from **[X]% to [Y]%** —
a gain of **[Z] percentage points**.
Per capita, nothing else here comes close.

Much of the story is *conversion*, not just addition.
Victoria upgraded painted bike lanes to protected cycle tracks,
and replaced sharrows with traffic-controlled local street bikeways —
infrastructure that actually changes who feels comfortable riding.
In a compact city, the right corridors in the right places
move the numbers fast.

```{python}
#| label: fig-victoria-map
#| fig-cap: "Victoria cycling network, 2019 (grey) and new high-comfort segments added by 2024 (blue). Dissemination block centroids shaded by access status in 2024."

import matplotlib.image as mpimg

fig_path = FIGS / "victoria_map.png"
if fig_path.exists():
    img = mpimg.imread(fig_path)
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 7))
    ax.imshow(img)
    ax.axis("off")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
else:
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 7))
    ax.text(0.5, 0.5, "Map will appear here after running\nscripts/03_make_figures.py",
            ha="center", va="center", fontsize=13, color="#888",
            transform=ax.transAxes)
    ax.axis("off")
    ax.set_title("Victoria — network map placeholder", color="#aaa")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
```

---

### 🥯 Montreal: real gains, real network

Montreal has the largest absolute high-comfort network of the three cities.
And it kept growing.

Between 2019 and 2024, population-weighted access rose from
**[X]% to [Y]%**.
In raw kilometres, Montreal's gains dwarf Victoria's.

Central to this: the **REV** — Réseau Express Vélo.
A set of high-frequency protected lanes built explicitly as
cycling rapid transit, not recreational paths. That framing matters.

```{python}
#| label: fig-montreal-map
#| fig-cap: "Montreal cycling network, 2019 (grey) and new high-comfort segments added by 2024 (green). REV corridors visible as high-density additions in central arrondissements."

fig_path = FIGS / "montreal_map.png"
if fig_path.exists():
    img = mpimg.imread(fig_path)
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 7))
    ax.imshow(img)
    ax.axis("off")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
else:
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 7))
    ax.text(0.5, 0.5, "Map will appear here after running\nscripts/03_make_figures.py",
            ha="center", va="center", fontsize=13, color="#888",
            transform=ax.transAxes)
    ax.axis("off")
    ax.set_title("Montreal — network map placeholder", color="#aaa")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
```

The catch: Montreal is large and uneven.
Gains have concentrated in the central plateau and downtown.
Outer arrondissements have seen much less.
The city-wide number is real, but it masks a lot of geography.

---

### 🚧 Toronto: real progress, uneven delivery

Toronto did build during this period.
New cycle tracks appeared along key corridors,
and the city made genuine additions to its high-comfort network.

But for a city of 2.7 million, the population-weighted numbers
tell a more modest story: access moved from **[X]% to [Y]%** —
roughly **[Z] percentage points**.

```{python}
#| label: fig-toronto-map
#| fig-cap: "Toronto cycling network, 2019 (grey) and new high-comfort segments added by 2024 (orange). Additions are concentrated in the inner city."

fig_path = FIGS / "toronto_map.png"
if fig_path.exists():
    img = mpimg.imread(fig_path)
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 7))
    ax.imshow(img)
    ax.axis("off")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
else:
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 7))
    ax.text(0.5, 0.5, "Map will appear here after running\nscripts/03_make_figures.py",
            ha="center", va="center", fontsize=13, color="#888",
            transform=ax.transAxes)
    ax.axis("off")
    ax.set_title("Toronto — network map placeholder", color="#aaa")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
```

The infrastructure that got built matters.
The gap is in what didn't — routes that were planned,
then deferred, reduced, or removed under political pressure.
The data reflects both.

---

### The divergence

```{python}
#| label: fig-divergence
#| fig-cap: "Population-weighted access to high-comfort cycling infrastructure, 2019 and 2024, by city. Lines connect the same city across years."

# TODO: replace with actual values once access results are computed
# results = pd.read_csv(DATA / "access_results.csv")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 5))
ax.text(0.5, 0.5, "Populate with real data\n(scripts/02_compute_access.py)",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=13, color="#888", transform=ax.transAxes)
ax.axis("off")
ax.set_title("The gap is widening — placeholder", color="#aaa")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

| City | 2019 | 2024 | Change |
|------|------|------|--------|
| Victoria | —% | —% | +— pp |
| Montreal | —% | —% | +— pp |
| Toronto  | —% | —% | +— pp |

: Population-weighted access to high-comfort cycling infrastructure.
Percentage of residents within 300 m of a high-comfort segment.
{#tbl-results}

---

## 🔍 Conclusion

This isn't a story about resources or city size.
It's about political choices.

Victoria and Montreal built the kind of infrastructure
the evidence says works. Toronto made progress —
but the scale of what's needed still outpaces what got delivered.

That matters beyond cycling mode share.
High-comfort networks are a transit multiplier:

- 🚌 **They extend the walkshed** around transit stops —
  critical for first/last mile in medium-density areas
  where bus frequency alone can't close the gap
- 📉 **They reduce peak-hour vehicle pressure**
  on commute corridors
- 🙋 **They reach new riders** — people who won't ride on
  painted lanes but will ride on protected ones.
  That's the mechanism behind modal shift.

The CCND will be updated again.
When it is, the question is whether the gap
between cities like Victoria and Montreal
and cities like Toronto has kept widening —
or whether the laggards have finally started catching up.

Based on what this analysis shows,
there's no reason to expect catch-up
without a real change in what actually gets built.

---

## Appendix: data and code

All analysis code is in the `scripts/` directory.

1. `scripts/01_load_data.py` — load and classify CCND shapefiles
2. `scripts/02_compute_access.py` — compute DB-level access indicators
3. `scripts/03_make_figures.py` — generate maps and export figures

```{python}
#| label: tbl-session-info
#| tbl-cap: "Python package versions used in this analysis."
#| code-fold: true

import importlib.metadata

pkgs = ["geopandas", "pandas", "matplotlib", "contextily", "shapely", "numpy"]
rows = []
for pkg in pkgs:
    try:
        rows.append({"Package": pkg, "Version": importlib.metadata.version(pkg)})
    except importlib.metadata.PackageNotFoundError:
        rows.append({"Package": pkg, "Version": "not installed"})

pd.DataFrame(rows).style.hide(axis="index")
```
